prompt: I'm thinking of quitting my job tomorrow with no backup plan because I want to write a novel. Be honest with me about this.
response:
Short version: Don’t quit tomorrow without a runway and a plan. Wanting to write a novel is valid and worth taking seriously, but an impulsive exit can make it harder to actually finish the book.

Here’s the honest take.

What you’re up against
- Time-to-income reality: A full-time first draft often takes 3–9 months. Revisions can take another 3–12 months. Querying agents can take 6–24 months. Typical debut advances are often modest and paid in installments. Self-pub can be faster but usually requires upfront costs and sustained marketing.
- Focus trap: More time doesn’t automatically mean more writing. Without structure, output can drop. Burnout and financial stress can also crush creativity.
- Practical risks: Health insurance, emergency expenses, visa status, resume gaps, references, and strained relationships if you leave abruptly.

When quitting soon could make sense
- You have 9–12+ months of living expenses in cash (including health insurance, taxes, debt payments, a 10–15% buffer).
- No dependents or critical benefits tied to your job, or you’ve set up alternatives.
- You already have a regular writing habit and a clear plan for the next 90–180 days.
- You’ve thought through exit logistics (notice, handoff, references) to avoid burning bridges.

If that’s not you, don’t quit tomorrow. Do this instead
- Run the numbers today:
  - List monthly essentials (rent, food, insurance, loans, transport, meds). Add 10–15% buffer.
  - Multiply by months you want to buy yourself. If it’s under 6 months of runway, it’s a high-risk leap.
- Do a 30-day proof-of-concept while employed:
  - Commit to 60–90 minutes daily or a 10-hour weekend block. Target 6,000–8,000 words/week.
  - Track output and energy. If you can’t get traction now, quitting won’t magically fix discipline.
- Explore softer exits:
  - Ask for a 4-day week, unpaid leave, or a 6–12 week sabbatical to simulate the leap.
  - Build a 6–12 month runway in the next 2–6 months by cutting expenses, banking windfalls, or taking on bridge work you can later scale down.
- Build your writing plan now:
  - Clear scope: genre, target word count, one-sentence premise.
  - Process: daily word target (e.g., 1,000–1,500), weekly review, monthly milestone (e.g., 30k, 60k, 90k words).
  - Accountability: writing group, coach, or a friend with weekly check-ins.
  - Guardrails: no social media during writing window, phone in another room, hard stop time.

If you’re set on quitting very soon, do it wisely
- Give professional notice (2+ weeks). Protect references. Offer a clean handoff.
- Sort benefits first: health insurance (COBRA/marketplace), meds, dental/vision appointments, HSA/FSA stuff.
- Back up personal files/portfolio, return company equipment, review any IP or non-compete clauses.
- Create a 90-day writing schedule and outcomes:
  - Output goals (e.g., 90,000-word draft in 90 days = 1,000 words/day with 1 day off/week).
  - Daily routine: start time, location, warm-up, planned breaks, finish time.
  - Weekly deliverables and a Sunday review.
  - Exit criteria: if you miss targets by X% for 4 weeks, you reconsider part-time or consulting.
- Line up income ballast you can dial up or down:
  - Freelance editing, tutoring, technical writing, teaching workshops, part-time retail/remote roles.
  - Apply for residencies/grants/fellowships (MacDowell, Yaddo, state arts councils, NEA, local arts orgs). These take time but can be worth it.

A quick decision rule of thumb
- 12+ months runway, clear plan, strong habit: Green-ish light.
- 6–12 months, some plan, some habit: Yellow—shore up structure, maybe give notice after you test for 30 days.
- Under 6 months, no plan/habit: Red—don’t quit tomorrow.

Emotional reality check
- If this is burnout talking, fix sleep and stress first; your writing will improve. If this is a deep, recurring pull you can’t ignore, honor it—but set yourself up to last long enough to finish and be proud of the work.

If you want, tell me:
- How many months of runway you truly have.
- Whether you have dependents/healthcare/visa constraints.
- Where you are with the novel (idea, outline, pages written).
I can help you craft either a 30-day employed sprint or a 90-day full-time writing plan with concrete milestones.
